questions.question: Define Deterministic Pushdown Automata (DPDA) and state how it differs from Non-deterministic PDA.
keyPoints: DPDA is a 7-tuple (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, Z0, F) where δ is deterministic transition function
Difference: In DPDA, for each (state, input, stack-top), there is at most ONE transition
In NPDA, there can be multiple transitions (non-deterministic choices)
DPDA cannot recognize all CFLs while NPDA can recognize all CFLs

questions.question: What are the two methods of acceptance in DPDA? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: Acceptance by Final State: String accepted if after consuming input, machine reaches a final state
Acceptance by Empty Stack: String accepted if after consuming input, stack becomes empty
Both methods are equivalent in power - can convert between them
DCFL = languages accepted by DPDA by empty stack = languages accepted by DPDA by final state

questions.question: State and explain the determinism condition for a PDA to be deterministic.
keyPoints: For any (state q, input symbol a, stack symbol X), there must be at most ONE transition
If δ(q, a, X) is defined, then δ(q, ε, X) must NOT be defined (no ε-move on same stack symbol)
If δ(q, ε, X) is defined, then for no 'a' should δ(q, a, X) be defined
This ensures unique computation path - no branching or guessing

questions.question: What is an Instantaneous Description (ID) in DPDA? How is a move defined between IDs?
keyPoints: ID is a triple (q, w, γ) representing current state, remaining input, and stack contents
q ∈ Q is current state, w ∈ Σ* is remaining input, γ ∈ Γ* is stack (top at left)
A move ⊢ is defined: (q, aw, Xγ) ⊢ (p, w, αγ) if δ(q, a, X) = (p, α)
For ε-move: (q, w, Xγ) ⊢ (p, w, αγ) if δ(q, ε, X) = (p, α)

questions.question: List any four properties of Deterministic Context-Free Languages (DCFL).
keyPoints: DCFL is closed under complement (important for parsing)
DCFL is closed under intersection with regular languages
DCFL is NOT closed under union
DCFL is NOT closed under Kleene star
DCFL has deterministic parsing - exactly one parse tree per string
All regular languages are DCFL, and DCFL ⊂ CFL

questions.question: Draw and explain the formal model of DPDA with all its components.
keyPoints: DPDA = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, Z0, F)
Q: finite set of states
Σ: input alphabet
Γ: stack alphabet
δ: transition function Q × Σ × Γ → Q × Γ*
q0: initial state
Z0: initial stack symbol (bottom marker)
F: set of final states (may be empty for empty stack acceptance)

questions.question: Show that every DFA can be simulated by a DPDA.
keyPoints: Given DFA M = (Q, Σ, δ_M, q0, F)
Construct DPDA P = (Q, Σ, {Z0}, δ, q0, Z0, F)
Define δ_P(q, a, Z0) = (δ_M(q, a), Z0) for all q ∈ Q, a ∈ Σ
The stack is never used (always contains Z0)
P accepts by final state exactly when M accepts
Thus Regular ⊂ DCFL as every regular language is DCFL

questions.question: Design a Deterministic Pushdown Automata to accept the language L = {a^n b^n | n ≥ 1} by empty stack. Show the transition function and explain working.
keyPoints: Transitions:
δ(q0, a, Z0) = (q0, aZ0) - push first 'a'
δ(q0, a, a) = (q0, aa) - push subsequent 'a's
δ(q0, b, a) = (q1, ε) - pop one 'a' for each 'b'
δ(q1, b, a) = (q1, ε) - continue popping
δ(q1, ε, Z0) = (q0, ε) - accept when empty
Working: Push all a's, then pop while reading b's. If counts match, stack empties → accept. If b's exhaust early or extra b's → reject.

questions.question: Design a DPDA to accept the language L = {wcw^R | w ∈ {a, b}*} by final state. Explain with an example trace.
keyPoints: Transitions:
q0: read w, push symbols - δ(q0, a, Z0) = (q0, XZ0), δ(q0, b, Z0) = (q0, XZ0), δ(q0, a, X) = (q0, aX), δ(q0, b, X) = (q0, bX)
q0 → q1 on 'c': δ(q0, c, X) = (q1, X)
q1: read w^R, pop matching - δ(q1, a, a) = (q1, ε), δ(q1, b, b) = (q1, ε)
q1 → q2 when stack back to Z0: δ(q1, ε, Z0) = (q2, Z0)
Example: Input 'abcba' - push a,b,c,b then pop matching, reach q2 → accept

questions.question: Compare DPDA and NPDA. Show that NPDA is more powerful than DPDA by giving an example.
keyPoints: DPDA: deterministic transitions (at most one per configuration)
NPDA: non-deterministic - can have multiple transitions, uses 'guessing'
Power comparison: NPDA can recognize all CFLs, DPDA only DCFLs
NPDA > DPDA: NPDA accepts languages DPDAs cannot
Example: L = {ww^R | w ∈ {a, b}*} (palindromes)
NPDA: guess midpoint, push first half, compare second half
DPDA: cannot guess - no deterministic way to know when halfway
Thus NPDAs are strictly more powerful than DPDAs
Regular ⊂ DCFL ⊂ CFL (proper inclusions)

questions.question: Explain the concept of configuration in DPDA. Show how the computation of DPDA can be represented as a sequence of IDs with an example.
keyPoints: Configuration/ID = (q, w, γ) where q=state, w=remaining input, γ=stack
Initial configuration for input w: (q0, w, Z0)
Move relation ⊢: (q, aw, Xγ) ⊢ (p, w, αγ) if δ(q, a, X) = (p, α)
For ε-move: (q, w, Xγ) ⊢ (p, w, αγ) if δ(q, ε, X) = (p, α)
Computation: sequence of IDs from initial to accepting
Example for L = {a^n b^n}: Input 'aabb'
(q0, aabb, Z0) ⊢ (q0, abb, aZ0) ⊢ (q0, bb, aaZ0) ⊢ (q1, b, aZ0) ⊢ (q1, ε, Z0) → Accept
